content.content:
1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	### Why two repos
13	
14	The split exists to keep the *what* separate from the *why* and
15	*how*, while preserving both as durable history:
16	
17	- **App repo** holds the "what" — code, docs, notes; the artefact
18	  the project is producing.
19	- **Bot session repo** holds the "why" and "how" — the bot's
20	  reasoning, dead ends, and in-session context that produced
21	  each change.
22	- **Cross-referencing** the two via `ochid:` trailers (see below)
23	  produces a coherent narrative across time. Any future reader —
24	  human or bot — can land on a commit and jump to its session
25	  counterpart to recover the *why*.
26	- **Splitting** also keeps app history free of session noise
27	  while preserving session data instead of discarding it after
28	  the turn.
29	
30	### ochid trailers
31	
32	Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
33	counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
34	path followed by the changeID:
35	
36	- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
37	- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`
38	
39	`vc-x1 push` appends the correct trailer per repo automatically.
40	For manual commits, use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both
41	changeIDs (first line is app repo, second is `.claude`).
42	
43	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
44	
45	- App repo: `.` (project root)
46	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
47	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
48	
49	The bot session repo is this project's **`other-repo`** — the
50	path is set by `[workspace].other-repo` in `.vc-config.toml`
51	(default `.claude`, as used here). Concrete examples below use
52	`.claude` for readability; treat the path as configurable where
53	it appears in prose.
54	
55	## Working Directory
56	
57	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
58	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
59	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
60	command failures downstream.
61	
62	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
63	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
64	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1/notes/`). Long absolute
65	paths clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
66	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
67	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
68	not a stylistic choice.
69	
70	## Memory
71	
72	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
73	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
74	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
75	and it loses on discoverability:
76	
77	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
78	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
79	  (human or bot).
80	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
81	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
82	
83	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
84	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
85	
86	## Speculation marker
87	
88	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
89	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
90	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
91	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
92	tell the measured from the inferred.
93	
94	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
95	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
96	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
97	in the written record.
98	
99	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
100	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
101	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
102	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
103	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
104	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
105	
106	## Writing style: prefer sub-bullets
107	
108	Durable text the bot writes should itemize with sub-bullets
109	rather than run as large prose paragraphs. Applies to:
110	
111	- `notes/chores-*.md`, `notes/todo.md`, committed READMEs, design notes.
112	- Module `//!` docstrings, item `///` doc comments on traits / structs / functions / methods, and any other doc comment that runs more than a few sentences.
113	
114	Short intro sentences (1–3) at the top of a section / item are
115	fine; detail goes into hierarchical bullet lists.
116	
117	**Why:** easier to scan and cross-reference during review;
118	matches how the user reads and edits committed reference
119	material. A wall of prose hides structure that a nested list
120	makes visible — and the same is true inside doc comments,
121	where rustdoc renders the list cleanly.
122	
123	**How to apply:**
124	
125	- Default to bullet structure for any non-trivial section or
126	  doc comment. Reserve paragraphs for brief intros and
127	  occasional contextual framing.
128	- If a paragraph runs past ~3 sentences, look for the implicit
129	  list inside it and break it out.
130	- Design-decision entries benefit most — the *reason* /
131	  *what-was-rejected* / *how-it-applies* decomposition reads
132	  naturally as sub-bullets.
133	- Single-line doc comments on trivial methods stay one line —
134	  bullets are not mandatory, just *available* when content is
135	  long enough to warrant them.
136	
137	**Clap-derive args caveat.** Doc comments on `#[arg(...)]`
138	fields drive `--help` output. Clap reflows by default and
139	collapses bullets into running prose. Add
140	`#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on any field whose doc
141	comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on its own line in
142	the rendered help.
143	
144	## jj Basics
145	
146	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
147	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
148	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
149	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
150	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
151	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
152	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
153	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
154	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
155	  session because session data updates continuously.
156	
157	## vc-x1 Basics
158	
159	`vc-x1` is the workspace tool that wraps jj operations across the
160	app and bot session repos. Once installed, it should drive 99% of
161	the commit / push / finalize flow — direct `jj` is the fallback
162	when something goes off-rails.
163	
164	Subcommands in current use (vc-x1 0.41.0; run
165	`vc-x1 <sub> --help` for the authoritative flag set):
166	
167	- `vc-x1 sync` — fetch and verify (or apply) bookmark divergence
168	  across all workspace repos. See
169	  [Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync`](#pre-step-vc-x1-sync) below.
170	- `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — full commit + push + finalize flow
171	  with two interactive approval gates (review, then message).
172	  The primary command for shipping a step. See
173	  [Run `vc-x1 push`](#run-vc-x1-push) below.
174	- `vc-x1 finalize` — squash, set bookmark, and/or push a jj repo
175	  (every flag is opt-in). Used by `vc-x1 push` internally; also
176	  the manual fallback. See
177	  [Manual finalize fallback](#manual-finalize-fallback) below.
178	- `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` — print change IDs for the
179	  current working commit in each repo, one per line. Used to
180	  compose `ochid:` trailers when committing manually.
181	- `vc-x1 validate-desc` / `vc-x1 fix-desc` — verify (and repair)
182	  cross-repo commit descriptions, including `ochid:` trailers.
183	  `fix-desc` is dry-run by default.
184	- `vc-x1 desc` / `vc-x1 list` / `vc-x1 show` — read-only commit
185	  inspection helpers.
186	- `vc-x1 init` / `vc-x1 clone` / `vc-x1 symlink` — workspace
187	  bootstrap commands.
188	
189	The bot thinks `--scope` is an in-flight `vc-x1 push` flag
190	intended to let push work consistently across single-repo and
191	dual-repo workspaces; the default behavior remains dual-repo,
192	which is what this template demonstrates. When the user
193	references other in-flight flags, treat them as planned
194	additions until `vc-x1 push --help` lists them. For the
195	currently-shipped flag set, see
196	[Run `vc-x1 push`](#run-vc-x1-push) below.
197	
198	For the full command surface, run `vc-x1 --help` and
199	`vc-x1 <subcommand> --help`.
200	
201	## Commits
202	
203	### Per-repo commit commands
204	
205	When committing manually (i.e. not via `vc-x1 push`), use `-R`
206	(`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
207	relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the
208	verb/action visible at the start of the command.
209	
210	#### App repo
211	```
212	jj commit -m \
213	"title" \
214	-m "body
215	
216	ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
217	-R .
218	```
219	
220	#### Bot session repo
221	```
222	jj commit -m \
223	"title" \
224	-m "body
225	
226	ochid: /<changeID>" \
227	-R .claude
228	```
229	
230	(See [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers) under Project Structure
231	for what the trailer is and why.)
232	
233	### Commit message style
234	
235	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
236	a version suffix:
237	
238	```
239	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
240	```
241	
242	- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
243	  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
244	  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
245	- **Body** (same across both repos): short intro paragraph (1–3
246	  sentences), then a terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds
247	  one-to-one with the edits structure already documented in
248	  `notes/chores-*.md` for this step — just the file and a
249	  one-line gist (e.g. `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not*
250	  restate the detail that lives in chores; the commit body is a
251	  scan-able index, not a duplicate. The chores section is the
252	  source of truth.
253	- **Session notes** (optional tail, appended to the body): if the
254	  session produced items worth preserving that don't fit the
255	  code-change index — in-session tooling work, dead ends,
256	  rationale — append a trailing subsection titled
257	  `## <other-repo> session notes:` (e.g.
258	  `## .claude session notes:`) with those items as bullets. The
259	  same body (main content plus any session-notes subsection)
260	  lands in both commits.
261	- **`ochid` trailer**: do *not* include `ochid:` in the body you
262	  pass via `--body`. `vc-x1 push` appends the correct per-repo
263	  trailer at the commit stages
264	  (`ochid: /.claude/<chid>` for the app-repo commit;
265	  `ochid: /<chid>` for the `.claude` commit). When committing
266	  manually, append the trailer yourself per the templates above.
267	- Examples:
268	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
269	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
270	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
271	
272	### Chores section headers
273	
274	Chores section headers use trailing version format:
275	
276	```
277	## Description (X.Y.Z)
278	```
279	
280	Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``
281	
282	### Notes references
283	
284	Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
285	See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.
286	
287	### Markdown anchor links
288	
289	GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
290	characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
291	**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
292	on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
293	`:`). General markdown reference:
294	[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
295	publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
296	de-facto reference implementation is
297	[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).
298	
299	## Code Conventions
300	
301	### Doc comments on every file, function, and method
302	
303	Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
304	function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
305	one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
306	comment exists, not that it be long.
307	
308	This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
309	default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
310	module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
311	inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
312	non-obvious WHY.
313	
314	For longer doc comments, follow
315	[Writing style: prefer sub-bullets](#writing-style-prefer-sub-bullets)
316	— short intro then a bullet list, with the `verbatim_doc_comment`
317	caveat for clap-derive args.
318	
319	### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)
320	
321	Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
322	`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
323	`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.
324	
325	- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
326	  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
327	  self-evident.
328	- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
329	  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).
330	
331	Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
332	Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
333	ring for readers seeing the code out of context.
334	
335	For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
336	`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
337	the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.
338	
339	```rust
340	let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
341	let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious
342	
343	match matches.len() {
344	    1 => {
345	        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
346	        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
347	        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
348	    }
349	    // ...
350	}
351	```
352	
353	Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
354	correct test behavior.
355	
356	### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input
357	
358	When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
359	ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
360	result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
361	redoing misaligned work costs much more.
362	
363	### Recognize when stuck
364	
365	If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
366	without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
367	requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
368	Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
369	on X."
370	
371	## Workflow
372	
373	The end-to-end flow for a step is: **edit → review → commit → push
374	→ finalize**, executed via `vc-x1 push` with two interactive
375	approval gates (review, message). Finalize is launched detached as
376	the absolute last action in the turn so trailing bot writes land in
377	the session repo.
378	
379	**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
380	Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a
381	single-step change is one `push` invocation; a multi-step change
382	is one `push` per `X.Y.Z-N` commit plus one for the final release
383	commit. Each step gets its own commit, its own push, and its own
384	finalize — so dev markers land on the remote and in `.claude` as
385	they happen rather than being batched until the end.
386	
387	### User approval points
388	
389	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without
390	the user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first;
391	only run them after the user confirms. This applies to late
392	changes too — pause for review before squashing into an existing
393	commit. The two approval gates surfaced by `vc-x1 push` (review,
394	message) are part of this discipline, not a substitute for it.
395	
396	### Review before proposing the commit block
397	
398	After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
399	there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the commit / push commands.
400	Wait for the user to signal review is complete before proposing
401	the commit block. Changes during review are the norm, not the
402	exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise and
403	signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.
404	
405	This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets
406	a review pause before its commit block appears.
407	
408	Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
409	commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
410	next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
411	previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
412	step before starting the next; don't ask.
413	
414	### Versioning
415	
416	Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
417	on scope:
418	
419	- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
420	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
421	- **Multi-step** (for scope that splits naturally into
422	  independently-reviewable steps): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`, implement
423	  across multiple `X.Y.Z-N` commits incrementing the numeric
424	  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix to close the ladder.
425	
426	The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
427	starting.
428	
429	For multi-step:
430	
431	1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
432	   marker.
433	2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
434	   needed). Every `X.Y.Z-N` commit is a complete checkpoint in
435	   its own right — the `-N` suffix only signals that more steps
436	   in the same ladder are coming, not that the commit is partial
437	   or exploratory. Run the full pre-commit checklist (including
438	   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores-*.md` updates) on every
439	   `-N` commit exactly as you would on a single-step release.
440	3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) to close the ladder.
441	
442	Multi-step cycles surface the ladder at the top of
443	`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` as a bullet list with `(done)` /
444	`(current)` markers — see the file's intro paragraph for the
445	format. Update the markers as each step ships so the In Progress
446	view stays current at a glance.
447	
448	**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
449	semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
450	component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
451	`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the ladder:
452	each `-N` sorts before its final unsuffixed sibling. Cargo
453	accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix adds no information the
454	git log doesn't already convey and doubles typing per commit.
455	
456	The final release commit (no suffix) closes the ladder rather
457	than amending prior commits. This keeps history readable: a
458	chain of complete checkpoints ending in an `X.Y.Z` marker.
459	
460	### Pre-commit checklist
461	
462	Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:
463	
464	1. `cargo check`
465	2. `cargo fmt`
466	3. `cargo clippy`
467	4. `cargo test`
468	5. `cargo test --release`
469	6. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
470	   is required, **not optional**. See
471	   [notes/cargo-locked-issue.md](notes/cargo-locked-issue.md) for
472	   the full story; short version: plain `cargo install` ignores
473	   `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves dependencies from scratch, which
474	   can pick incompatible versions even when `cargo build` /
475	   `cargo test` succeed.
476	7. Retest after install
477	8. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
478	9. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
479	10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
480	   new subcommands, changed behavior)
481	
482	Push's preflight re-runs `vc-x1 sync` + `cargo fmt` +
483	`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` + `cargo test` as a
484	safety net. Run the checklist above locally anyway — fmt / clippy /
485	test iteration is cheaper in-conversation than bouncing off
486	preflight after review approval. Steps 6–7 (`cargo install --path .`
487	and retest) are **not** in push's preflight (project-specific); the
488	bot runs them.
489	
490	### Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync`
491	
492	Push's preflight runs `vc-x1 sync --check` as its first step. Check
493	mode verifies divergence without applying — if anything is `behind`
494	or `diverged`, preflight errors and you resolve explicitly with
495	`vc-x1 sync --no-check` before re-running push. This keeps the
496	two approval gates (review, message) authoritative; push never
497	performs an unsupervised rebase.
498	
499	Running sync manually before you *start* editing is still cheap
500	(one line when clean) and surfaces remote changes earlier:
501	
502	```
503	vc-x1 sync --check                    # dual-repo workspace, verify
504	vc-x1 sync --no-check                 # apply (rebase / fast-forward)
505	vc-x1 sync --check -R .               # single-repo project
506	vc-x1 sync --check --quiet            # silent; exit code signals result
507	```
508	
509	Bots and scripts must pass `--check` or `--no-check` explicitly —
510	defaults can shift, explicit flags lock in the contract. Interactive
511	use can take the default (which is `--check`).
512	
513	Output shape:
514	
515	- **Clean**: one line — `sync: N repos, all bookmarks up-to-date`.
516	  Note: scope is bookmark-vs-remote tracking, not working-copy
517	  cleanliness — `@` may have uncommitted changes; sync doesn't
518	  speak to that. Proceed.
519	- **Action needed** (`behind` / `diverged`) under `--check`:
520	  per-repo fetch + state lines, then a fatal
521	  `sync: N repos need action — resolve with vc-x1 sync --no-check
522	  and re-run`. Inspect, run `vc-x1 sync --no-check`, then proceed.
523	- **`--quiet`**: no output; exit code is the only signal.
524	
525	### Run `vc-x1 push`
526	
527	**Prerequisites — one-time per repo:**
528	
529	- `.gitignore` must include `/.vc-x1` so push's in-progress
530	  state files (`.vc-x1/push-state.toml`) don't get committed.
531	  Push warns at preflight if the entry is missing.
532	- The target bookmark must have a tracking remote
533	  (`jj bookmark track <bookmark>@origin -R .`). `jj git fetch`
534	  creates remote bookmarks as non-tracking by default; push
535	  currently surfaces this as a raw jj error at `push-app` after
536	  commits are locally made, so confirm tracking is set up
537	  before the first push in a new clone.
538	
539	**`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps the full flow in one command with
540	two interactive approval gates.** Push runs preflight (sync + fmt
541	+ clippy + test), prompts for review, composes the commit message
542	(via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`), prompts for message
543	approval, then commits both repos → advances bookmarks → pushes
544	app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the local mutation
545	window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`; after `push-app`
546	succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and recovery is
547	forward-only.
548	
549	```
550	vc-x1 push main                                      # interactive (review + $EDITOR)
551	vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."           # flags skip $EDITOR
552	vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."     # full non-interactive
553	vc-x1 push main --dry-run                            # preview (no side effects)
554	vc-x1 push main --from commit-app                    # resume from specific stage
555	vc-x1 push --status                                  # show saved state
556	vc-x1 push main --restart                            # clear saved state; start fresh
557	```
558	
559	The two approval gates are surfaced by push itself:
560	
561	1. **Review** — push prints `jj diff --stat` for both repos and
562	   prompts `[y/N]`. Approve = "the work is done right".
563	2. **Message** — push either uses `--title`/`--body` (non-editor
564	   path) or opens `$EDITOR` on a template. Approve = "the message
565	   reads right".
566	
567	Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits;
568	per-repo `ochid:` trailers are appended by push itself. See
569	[Commit message style](#commit-message-style) for body structure.
570	
571	For the full flag list and stage machine, see `vc-x1 push --help`.
572	
573	### Bot communication during the flow
574	
575	When applying the flow on the user's behalf, use plain prose at
576	each gate — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N",
577	etc.):
578	
579	1. **After completing the work** — summarize what changed
580	   (file-by-file or feature-by-feature, terse) and end with
581	   something like:
582	
583	   > Work complete. Please review. On approval I'll prep the
584	   > commit title and body for a second approval before pushing.
585	
586	2. **After review approval** — present the proposed `$TITLE` and
587	   `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to run `vc-x1 push`.
588	   Do **not** spell out the full
589	   `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"`
590	   invocation by default — it's mechanical and obvious from the
591	   title/body. Show it only on explicit request (verbose mode /
592	   debugging).
593	
594	3. **After execution approval** — run the push command. `push`
595	   handles commit + bookmark + push + finalize internally;
596	   nothing should be output after the push command (finalize is
597	   detached and absolute-last; see
598	   [Finalize is the absolute last action](#finalize-is-the-absolute-last-action)
599	   below).
600	
601	### Late changes after push
602	
603	If the app repo needs a tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
604	updating CLAUDE.md or memory), the commit is immutable. Use
605	`--ignore-immutable` to squash the changes into `@-` — the
606	bookmark moves with the rewritten commit — then re-push:
607	
608	```
609	jj squash --ignore-immutable -R .
610	jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
611	```
612	
613	If you squash somewhere other than `@-`, add
614	`jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r <target> -R .` between those
615	two commands so the bookmark lands on the rewritten commit.
616	
617	`.claude` is also mutable via this pattern when needed, though
618	push's `finalize-claude` stage normally handles trailing session
619	writes so you rarely hit this case there.
620	
621	### Finalize is the absolute last action
622	
623	`vc-x1 finalize` runs detached with `--squash` and a short
624	`--delay` so any writes the bot makes immediately after launching
625	finalize still land in the squash window and end up in the
626	session-repo commit. **That guarantee depends on finalize being
627	the last thing in the turn.**
628	
629	After `vc-x1 finalize` is launched — **whether mid-session
630	per-step or at session end** — you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a
631	next step, edit files, run tools, or emit any text (prose,
632	recaps, acknowledgements), until the user explicitly directs you
633	to continue. Treat finalize as a hard stop for the whole turn.
634	Any final words (e.g. "next is ...") must be said in the
635	approval prompt *before* executing finalize; the finalize `Bash`
636	call is the last thing in the turn and nothing follows it.
637	
638	This holds even when the next step seems obvious (e.g. "next is
639	N+1" or "now I should bump the version and commit the release").
640	Wait. The user controls cadence — every push+finalize is a
641	checkpoint they may want to inspect, think about, hand off, or
642	take a break at. Auto-proceeding bypasses that checkpoint and
643	produces unwanted writes between finalize and the next explicit
644	instruction.
645	
646	Do **not** echo or restate the finalize output — the Bash tool
647	already displays it. Any trailing text creates writes that miss
648	the finalize squash window.
649	
650	Exceptions to this rule may emerge later but are not authorized
651	at this stage. Until told otherwise, treat as absolute.
652	
653	### Manual finalize fallback
654	
655	If push exited before `finalize-claude` (e.g. `--no-finalize`
656	was set, or a failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`),
657	run finalize by hand:
658	
659	```
660	vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
661	```
662	
663	The same "finalize is the absolute last action" rule applies —
664	nothing should happen after finalize. If any work is done after
665	finalize, run finalize again so the trailing writes are captured.
666	
667	**Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery** —
668	manual `vc-x1 finalize`, manual `jj squash --ignore-immutable`
669	+ force-push, etc., all leave `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` pointing
670	at a now-stale halt point. Either `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml`
671	or run `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --restart` (which clears and
672	restarts in one go) before the next `vc-x1 push`. Otherwise
673	push resumes from a bogus stage and can falsely declare
674	success.
675

file.content:
# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions

## Project Structure

This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:

1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.

Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.

### Why two repos

The split exists to keep the *what* separate from the *why* and
*how*, while preserving both as durable history:

- **App repo** holds the "what" — code, docs, notes; the artefact
  the project is producing.
- **Bot session repo** holds the "why" and "how" — the bot's
  reasoning, dead ends, and in-session context that produced
  each change.
- **Cross-referencing** the two via `ochid:` trailers (see below)
  produces a coherent narrative across time. Any future reader —
  human or bot — can land on a commit and jump to its session
  counterpart to recover the *why*.
- **Splitting** also keeps app history free of session noise
  while preserving session data instead of discarding it after
  the turn.

### ochid trailers

Every commit body must include an `ochid:` trailer pointing to the
counterpart commit in the other repo. The value is a workspace-root-relative
path followed by the changeID:

- App repo commits point to `.claude`: `ochid: /.claude/<changeID>`
- Bot session commits point to app repo: `ochid: /<changeID>`

`vc-x1 push` appends the correct trailer per repo automatically.
For manual commits, use `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` to get both
changeIDs (first line is app repo, second is `.claude`).

## Repo Paths (relative from project root)

- App repo: `.` (project root)
- Bot session repo: `.claude`
  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)

The bot session repo is this project's **`other-repo`** — the
path is set by `[workspace].other-repo` in `.vc-config.toml`
(default `.claude`, as used here). Concrete examples below use
`.claude` for readability; treat the path as configurable where
it appears in prose.

## Working Directory

Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
command failures downstream.

When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-template-x1/notes/`). Long absolute
paths clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
not a stylistic choice.

## Memory

Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
and it loses on discoverability:

- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
  (human or bot).
- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.

Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.

## Speculation marker

Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
tell the measured from the inferred.

**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
in the written record.

**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.

## Writing style: prefer sub-bullets

Durable text the bot writes should itemize with sub-bullets
rather than run as large prose paragraphs. Applies to:

- `notes/chores-*.md`, `notes/todo.md`, committed READMEs, design notes.
- Module `//!` docstrings, item `///` doc comments on traits / structs / functions / methods, and any other doc comment that runs more than a few sentences.

Short intro sentences (1–3) at the top of a section / item are
fine; detail goes into hierarchical bullet lists.

**Why:** easier to scan and cross-reference during review;
matches how the user reads and edits committed reference
material. A wall of prose hides structure that a nested list
makes visible — and the same is true inside doc comments,
where rustdoc renders the list cleanly.

**How to apply:**

- Default to bullet structure for any non-trivial section or
  doc comment. Reserve paragraphs for brief intros and
  occasional contextual framing.
- If a paragraph runs past ~3 sentences, look for the implicit
  list inside it and break it out.
- Design-decision entries benefit most — the *reason* /
  *what-was-rejected* / *how-it-applies* decomposition reads
  naturally as sub-bullets.
- Single-line doc comments on trivial methods stay one line —
  bullets are not mandatory, just *available* when content is
  long enough to warrant them.

**Clap-derive args caveat.** Doc comments on `#[arg(...)]`
fields drive `--help` output. Clap reflows by default and
collapses bullets into running prose. Add
`#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on any field whose doc
comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on its own line in
the rendered help.

## jj Basics

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.

## vc-x1 Basics

`vc-x1` is the workspace tool that wraps jj operations across the
app and bot session repos. Once installed, it should drive 99% of
the commit / push / finalize flow — direct `jj` is the fallback
when something goes off-rails.

Subcommands in current use (vc-x1 0.41.0; run
`vc-x1 <sub> --help` for the authoritative flag set):

- `vc-x1 sync` — fetch and verify (or apply) bookmark divergence
  across all workspace repos. See
  [Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync`](#pre-step-vc-x1-sync) below.
- `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` — full commit + push + finalize flow
  with two interactive approval gates (review, then message).
  The primary command for shipping a step. See
  [Run `vc-x1 push`](#run-vc-x1-push) below.
- `vc-x1 finalize` — squash, set bookmark, and/or push a jj repo
  (every flag is opt-in). Used by `vc-x1 push` internally; also
  the manual fallback. See
  [Manual finalize fallback](#manual-finalize-fallback) below.
- `vc-x1 chid -R .,.claude -L` — print change IDs for the
  current working commit in each repo, one per line. Used to
  compose `ochid:` trailers when committing manually.
- `vc-x1 validate-desc` / `vc-x1 fix-desc` — verify (and repair)
  cross-repo commit descriptions, including `ochid:` trailers.
  `fix-desc` is dry-run by default.
- `vc-x1 desc` / `vc-x1 list` / `vc-x1 show` — read-only commit
  inspection helpers.
- `vc-x1 init` / `vc-x1 clone` / `vc-x1 symlink` — workspace
  bootstrap commands.

The bot thinks `--scope` is an in-flight `vc-x1 push` flag
intended to let push work consistently across single-repo and
dual-repo workspaces; the default behavior remains dual-repo,
which is what this template demonstrates. When the user
references other in-flight flags, treat them as planned
additions until `vc-x1 push --help` lists them. For the
currently-shipped flag set, see
[Run `vc-x1 push`](#run-vc-x1-push) below.

For the full command surface, run `vc-x1 --help` and
`vc-x1 <subcommand> --help`.

## Commits

### Per-repo commit commands

When committing manually (i.e. not via `vc-x1 push`), use `-R`
(`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the
verb/action visible at the start of the command.

#### App repo
```
jj commit -m \
"title" \
-m "body

ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
-R .
```

#### Bot session repo
```
jj commit -m \
"title" \
-m "body

ochid: /<changeID>" \
-R .claude
```

(See [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers) under Project Structure
for what the trailer is and why.)

### Commit message style

Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
- **Body** (same across both repos): short intro paragraph (1–3
  sentences), then a terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds
  one-to-one with the edits structure already documented in
  `notes/chores-*.md` for this step — just the file and a
  one-line gist (e.g. `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not*
  restate the detail that lives in chores; the commit body is a
  scan-able index, not a duplicate. The chores section is the
  source of truth.
- **Session notes** (optional tail, appended to the body): if the
  session produced items worth preserving that don't fit the
  code-change index — in-session tooling work, dead ends,
  rationale — append a trailing subsection titled
  `## <other-repo> session notes:` (e.g.
  `## .claude session notes:`) with those items as bullets. The
  same body (main content plus any session-notes subsection)
  lands in both commits.
- **`ochid` trailer**: do *not* include `ochid:` in the body you
  pass via `--body`. `vc-x1 push` appends the correct per-repo
  trailer at the commit stages
  (`ochid: /.claude/<chid>` for the app-repo commit;
  `ochid: /<chid>` for the `.claude` commit). When committing
  manually, append the trailer yourself per the templates above.
- Examples:
  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`

### Chores section headers

Chores section headers use trailing version format:

```
## Description (X.Y.Z)
```

Example: `` ## Add `fn claude-symlink` (0.27.0) ``

### Notes references

Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.

### Markdown anchor links

GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
`:`). General markdown reference:
[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
de-facto reference implementation is
[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).

## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long.

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

For longer doc comments, follow
[Writing style: prefer sub-bullets](#writing-style-prefer-sub-bullets)
— short intro then a bullet list, with the `verbatim_doc_comment`
caveat for clap-derive args.

### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)

Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.

- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
  self-evident.
- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).

Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
ring for readers seeing the code out of context.

For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.

```rust
let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious

match matches.len() {
    1 => {
        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
    }
    // ...
}
```

Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
correct test behavior.

### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input

When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
redoing misaligned work costs much more.

### Recognize when stuck

If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
on X."

## Workflow

The end-to-end flow for a step is: **edit → review → commit → push
→ finalize**, executed via `vc-x1 push` with two interactive
approval gates (review, message). Finalize is launched detached as
the absolute last action in the turn so trailing bot writes land in
the session repo.

**Run this flow after every step** — not only at session end.
Single-step and multi-step changes are of equal importance: a
single-step change is one `push` invocation; a multi-step change
is one `push` per `X.Y.Z-N` commit plus one for the final release
commit. Each step gets its own commit, its own push, and its own
finalize — so dev markers land on the remote and in `.claude` as
they happen rather than being batched until the end.

### User approval points

Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without
the user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first;
only run them after the user confirms. This applies to late
changes too — pause for review before squashing into an existing
commit. The two approval gates surfaced by `vc-x1 push` (review,
message) are part of this discipline, not a substitute for it.

### Review before proposing the commit block

After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the commit / push commands.
Wait for the user to signal review is complete before proposing
the commit block. Changes during review are the norm, not the
exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise and
signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.

This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets
a review pause before its commit block appears.

Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
step before starting the next; don't ask.

### Versioning

Every plan must start with a version bump. Choose the approach based
on scope:

- **Single-step** (recommended for mechanical/focused changes): bump
  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
- **Multi-step** (for scope that splits naturally into
  independently-reviewable steps): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`, implement
  across multiple `X.Y.Z-N` commits incrementing the numeric
  suffix. The final commit drops the suffix to close the ladder.

The plan should recommend one approach and get user approval before
starting.

For multi-step:

1. Bump version to `X.Y.Z-0` with the plan and commit as a chore
   marker.
2. Implement in one or more `X.Y.Z-N` commits (increment N as
   needed). Every `X.Y.Z-N` commit is a complete checkpoint in
   its own right — the `-N` suffix only signals that more steps
   in the same ladder are coming, not that the commit is partial
   or exploratory. Run the full pre-commit checklist (including
   `notes/todo.md` and `notes/chores-*.md` updates) on every
   `-N` commit exactly as you would on a single-step release.
3. Final commit bumps to `X.Y.Z` (no suffix) to close the ladder.

Multi-step cycles surface the ladder at the top of
`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress` as a bullet list with `(done)` /
`(current)` markers — see the file's intro paragraph for the
format. Update the markers as each step ships so the In Progress
view stays current at a glance.

**Why numeric suffixes (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:**
semver pre-release identifiers may consist of a single numeric
component, and they compare numerically per spec. So
`X.Y.Z-1 < X.Y.Z-2 < … < X.Y.Z` correctly orders the ladder:
each `-N` sorts before its final unsuffixed sibling. Cargo
accepts this form. The `-dev` prefix adds no information the
git log doesn't already convey and doubles typing per commit.

The final release commit (no suffix) closes the ladder rather
than amending prior commits. This keeps history readable: a
chain of complete checkpoints ending in an `X.Y.Z` marker.

### Pre-commit checklist

Before proposing a commit, run all of the following and fix any issues:

1. `cargo check`
2. `cargo fmt`
3. `cargo clippy`
4. `cargo test`
5. `cargo test --release`
6. `cargo install --path . --locked` (if applicable) — `--locked`
   is required, **not optional**. See
   [notes/cargo-locked-issue.md](notes/cargo-locked-issue.md) for
   the full story; short version: plain `cargo install` ignores
   `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves dependencies from scratch, which
   can pick incompatible versions even when `cargo build` /
   `cargo test` succeed.
7. Retest after install
8. Update `notes/todo.md` — add to `## Done` if completing a task
9. Update `notes/chores-*.md` — add a subsection describing the change
10. Update `notes/README.md` — if functionality changed (new flags,
   new subcommands, changed behavior)

Push's preflight re-runs `vc-x1 sync` + `cargo fmt` +
`cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` + `cargo test` as a
safety net. Run the checklist above locally anyway — fmt / clippy /
test iteration is cheaper in-conversation than bouncing off
preflight after review approval. Steps 6–7 (`cargo install --path .`
and retest) are **not** in push's preflight (project-specific); the
bot runs them.

### Pre-step: `vc-x1 sync`

Push's preflight runs `vc-x1 sync --check` as its first step. Check
mode verifies divergence without applying — if anything is `behind`
or `diverged`, preflight errors and you resolve explicitly with
`vc-x1 sync --no-check` before re-running push. This keeps the
two approval gates (review, message) authoritative; push never
performs an unsupervised rebase.

Running sync manually before you *start* editing is still cheap
(one line when clean) and surfaces remote changes earlier:

```
vc-x1 sync --check                    # dual-repo workspace, verify
vc-x1 sync --no-check                 # apply (rebase / fast-forward)
vc-x1 sync --check -R .               # single-repo project
vc-x1 sync --check --quiet            # silent; exit code signals result
```

Bots and scripts must pass `--check` or `--no-check` explicitly —
defaults can shift, explicit flags lock in the contract. Interactive
use can take the default (which is `--check`).

Output shape:

- **Clean**: one line — `sync: N repos, all bookmarks up-to-date`.
  Note: scope is bookmark-vs-remote tracking, not working-copy
  cleanliness — `@` may have uncommitted changes; sync doesn't
  speak to that. Proceed.
- **Action needed** (`behind` / `diverged`) under `--check`:
  per-repo fetch + state lines, then a fatal
  `sync: N repos need action — resolve with vc-x1 sync --no-check
  and re-run`. Inspect, run `vc-x1 sync --no-check`, then proceed.
- **`--quiet`**: no output; exit code is the only signal.

### Run `vc-x1 push`

**Prerequisites — one-time per repo:**

- `.gitignore` must include `/.vc-x1` so push's in-progress
  state files (`.vc-x1/push-state.toml`) don't get committed.
  Push warns at preflight if the entry is missing.
- The target bookmark must have a tracking remote
  (`jj bookmark track <bookmark>@origin -R .`). `jj git fetch`
  creates remote bookmarks as non-tracking by default; push
  currently surfaces this as a raw jj error at `push-app` after
  commits are locally made, so confirm tracking is set up
  before the first push in a new clone.

**`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps the full flow in one command with
two interactive approval gates.** Push runs preflight (sync + fmt
+ clippy + test), prompts for review, composes the commit message
(via `--title`/`--body` or `$EDITOR`), prompts for message
approval, then commits both repos → advances bookmarks → pushes
app → finalizes `.claude`. Failures inside the local mutation
window roll both repos back via `jj op restore`; after `push-app`
succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and recovery is
forward-only.

```
vc-x1 push main                                      # interactive (review + $EDITOR)
vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."           # flags skip $EDITOR
vc-x1 push main --yes --title "..." --body "..."     # full non-interactive
vc-x1 push main --dry-run                            # preview (no side effects)
vc-x1 push main --from commit-app                    # resume from specific stage
vc-x1 push --status                                  # show saved state
vc-x1 push main --restart                            # clear saved state; start fresh
```

The two approval gates are surfaced by push itself:

1. **Review** — push prints `jj diff --stat` for both repos and
   prompts `[y/N]`. Approve = "the work is done right".
2. **Message** — push either uses `--title`/`--body` (non-editor
   path) or opens `$EDITOR` on a template. Approve = "the message
   reads right".

Both titles and bodies are the **same** across the two commits;
per-repo `ochid:` trailers are appended by push itself. See
[Commit message style](#commit-message-style) for body structure.

For the full flag list and stage machine, see `vc-x1 push --help`.

### Bot communication during the flow

When applying the flow on the user's behalf, use plain prose at
each gate — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N",
etc.):

1. **After completing the work** — summarize what changed
   (file-by-file or feature-by-feature, terse) and end with
   something like:

   > Work complete. Please review. On approval I'll prep the
   > commit title and body for a second approval before pushing.

2. **After review approval** — present the proposed `$TITLE` and
   `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to run `vc-x1 push`.
   Do **not** spell out the full
   `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"`
   invocation by default — it's mechanical and obvious from the
   title/body. Show it only on explicit request (verbose mode /
   debugging).

3. **After execution approval** — run the push command. `push`
   handles commit + bookmark + push + finalize internally;
   nothing should be output after the push command (finalize is
   detached and absolute-last; see
   [Finalize is the absolute last action](#finalize-is-the-absolute-last-action)
   below).

### Late changes after push

If the app repo needs a tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory), the commit is immutable. Use
`--ignore-immutable` to squash the changes into `@-` — the
bookmark moves with the rewritten commit — then re-push:

```
jj squash --ignore-immutable -R .
jj git push --bookmark <bookmark> -R .
```

If you squash somewhere other than `@-`, add
`jj bookmark set <bookmark> -r <target> -R .` between those
two commands so the bookmark lands on the rewritten commit.

`.claude` is also mutable via this pattern when needed, though
push's `finalize-claude` stage normally handles trailing session
writes so you rarely hit this case there.

### Finalize is the absolute last action

`vc-x1 finalize` runs detached with `--squash` and a short
`--delay` so any writes the bot makes immediately after launching
finalize still land in the squash window and end up in the
session-repo commit. **That guarantee depends on finalize being
the last thing in the turn.**

After `vc-x1 finalize` is launched — **whether mid-session
per-step or at session end** — you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a
next step, edit files, run tools, or emit any text (prose,
recaps, acknowledgements), until the user explicitly directs you
to continue. Treat finalize as a hard stop for the whole turn.
Any final words (e.g. "next is ...") must be said in the
approval prompt *before* executing finalize; the finalize `Bash`
call is the last thing in the turn and nothing follows it.

This holds even when the next step seems obvious (e.g. "next is
N+1" or "now I should bump the version and commit the release").
Wait. The user controls cadence — every push+finalize is a
checkpoint they may want to inspect, think about, hand off, or
take a break at. Auto-proceeding bypasses that checkpoint and
produces unwanted writes between finalize and the next explicit
instruction.

Do **not** echo or restate the finalize output — the Bash tool
already displays it. Any trailing text creates writes that miss
the finalize squash window.

Exceptions to this rule may emerge later but are not authorized
at this stage. Until told otherwise, treat as absolute.

### Manual finalize fallback

If push exited before `finalize-claude` (e.g. `--no-finalize`
was set, or a failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`),
run finalize by hand:

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```

The same "finalize is the absolute last action" rule applies —
nothing should happen after finalize. If any work is done after
finalize, run finalize again so the trailing writes are captured.

**Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery** —
manual `vc-x1 finalize`, manual `jj squash --ignore-immutable`
+ force-push, etc., all leave `.vc-x1/push-state.toml` pointing
at a now-stale halt point. Either `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml`
or run `vc-x1 push <bookmark> --restart` (which clears and
restarts in one go) before the next `vc-x1 push`. Otherwise
push resumes from a bogus stage and can falsely declare
success.
